body_txt: In Real Life , autism (now officially referred to in the DSM as Autism Spectrum Disorder, or ASD) is a complex spectrum of forms of neurodivergence that can affect an autistic individual's social interactions, among other areas as detailed in our SoYouWantTo.Portray Autism Accurately page. Many autistic people are quite knowledgeable about certain subjects about which they are passionate, termed special interests. In spite of most Hollywood portrayals displaying autistic people as being gifted in math, science, or music, the interests and talents of real-life autistic people vary. Not all autistic people have one specific area of talent or interest — some are quite the Renaissance person . Additionally, in most cases, any talents an autistic person has are the result of constant passion and practice — same as any neurotypical person — rather than being inherited excellence (Autistic savants do exist, but as of 2009, only take up 10% of the autistic population at most ). While there are more males than females diagnosed with autism, there are plenty of autistic females out there. Research shows that autism presents in all sexes at a relatively even rate, merely being more apparent in males due to the "only men can have autism" attitude. Autism lasts forever, and there are countless autistic adults who work, go to college, live on their own, and have healthy relationships. Furthermore, autistic people in Real Life are, well, actually real autistic people . In contrast, the stereotypical representation of autism, called Hollywood Autism , which is most likely to be portrayed as a white, cisgender, heterosexual male note Centers for Disease Control and Prevention postulates "socioeconomic or other barriers" may impede diagnosis in some communities , possessing a beanpole build ; and by a neurotypical - or non-autistic - actor, especially in Live-Action TV and Film and characters on the lower-support end of the spectrum (for logistical reasons). It is most common for an autistic character to be a child and if he is an adult, he's most likely to be the Idiot Savant , a creep, or simply a Manchild . He is portrayed as almost completely lacking emotions, empathy, and compassion. He either doesn't talk a lot or talks too much . Folks in his life think he's boring, annoying, nerdy, weird, or even creepy. Additionally, he is totally unable to live what most people would call a normal life and is ultimately a burden on those around him. Often this is depicted as being unable to handle various aspects of day-to-day life and having loud, even violent meltdowns on a regular basis. They are also portrayed by actors, ones who probably weren't picked for being autistic, or, considering the accuracy of their portrayal, knowing an autistic person. Most controversially , their lives are rarely depicted as being as fulfilling or as much of a life as that of someone who is not autistic. Although there have been more examples of autistic adults in media whose lives are depicted as non-tragic and even find romance and have children (unless they are content with being single and childfree), they are still far rarer than examples of children and adults whose autism is shown as tragic. While commonalities between most individuals do exist, real-life autism is much more complex than how this trope portrays it, branching out in a wide variety of ways from person to person, making this trope's Truth in Television status too questionable for real-life examples. Characters with Hollywood Autism are commonly Literal-Minded and may be Insufferable Geniuses . Not only do they get really into their interests, but these interests tend to be something no neurotypical person would ever be interested in , like naming every piece of a train engine or every station on a given line. In contrast, they will often show zero interest in sex, relationships, sports, and other " normal " mainstream interests. This character may also be a Cloudcuckoolander or have Attention Deficit... Ooh, Shiny! note ASD often exhibits with other disorders, such as ADHD, but not always . For some reason, this facet of autism in pop culture conveniently ignores that some stereotypical interests for people on the spectrum - namely planes, trains, trucks, space, military equipment and media (particularly animation and video games) - are some of the most common interests on the planet, and are enjoyed just as much by neurotypicals as they are by autistic people. If this type of character has Animal Motifs , they're often associated with cats. This is a point of comparison that many autistic people and autism rights advocates actually endorse , owing to the bevy of behavioral similarities between cats and what is common among autistic individuals (including stimming or "self-stimulation", repeated motions for self-soothing note originally implying masturbation, coined by Dr. Ivar Lovaas , who used shock and other brutal means to "extinguish" autism (and homosexuality, in the Feminine Boy Project ; the technical word for stimming is stereotypy ) , special interests, adherence to routines, sensory sensitivity, and highly subtle and easily misunderstood/ overpathologized body language) to the point where the cat has become an unofficial mascot of sorts for the autism rights movement. A study by researchers at the University of Missouri even showed that cats can be the best pets for autistic children. Expect a Hollywood Autistic to be Inspirationally Disadvantaged or for his Hollywood Autism to be a Disability Superpower . He might also be part of a Five-Token Band , if he's being portrayed as the brains of the group or the token disabled character, and may even vacillate between one and the other. The difficulties inherent in depicting autism and other neurodivergent conditions are one reason for the popularity of not specifying the condition, which gives creators more leeway in interpretation. If those characters were to be diagnosed according to DSM-5 criteria, most of them would probably get an ASD Level 2 ("Requiring substantial support") diagnosis note or even Level 3 ("Requiring very substantial support"), if they're shown as mostly non-verbal or with some form of cognitive impairment , with ASD Level 1 ("Requiring support") possibly matching some more downplayed examples. However, one can perfectly be a Level 2 or even Level 3 without being a stereotype of autism, nor, by definition, being an inaccurate portrayal. It’s worth noting that in large part due to autistic people gaining a louder voice in the public sphere in recent years, aversions of this trope have become increasingly common in media. Especially since the start of the 2020s, there has been an increased push for autistic characters on-screen to not always be cisgender, heterosexual white men. And while traditionally neurotypical actors have portrayed autistic characters in media, it’s become more common for actors who are themselves autistic to play autistic roles. However, as this trend is very recent, the majority of media depictions of autism are still heavily stereotypical. And for some individuals, many of these symptoms and qualities are truth in television. Be careful about using "high-functioning", "low-functioning" or "Asperger's" below. These are terms from nearly a century in the past when eugenics was more common in medicine. Dr. Asperger was interested in determining which "defective" individuals could be made to do useful work for the fatherland and which were dead weight to be disposed of. He and much of the German medical community helped the Nazi government sort the disabled into these groups, with some of the "useless" ones being sent to clinics for euthanasia or to Poland for mass extermination under Aktion T4, the alpha program that would be perfected for the Third Reich's later more famous genocides. "High functioning" and "low functioning" have been phased out because they pathologize the individual, replaced instead by language that emphasizes the amount of support they need, and rather than being a global term, the focus is on areas or tasks where they need that support (such as navigation for someone who's only difficulty is distinguishing left and right, or muted environments for someone with stronger sensitivity issues, to full time care for someone who can't maintain any kind of employment). See also "L" Is for "Dyslexia" , Hollywood Tourette's , and Hollywood Intellectual Disability for other inaccurate portrayals of mental disabilities. An autistic victim of Neurodivergence Stigma might be assumed to be like this by people whose ideas of autism are rooted in stereotypes. noreallife
